summary:
Post-injury inflammation begins promptly via a neutrophil-driven immune response aimed at clearing debris and preventing infection, marking it as the first observable transient change. Key cells involved are early-arriving neutrophils that migrate toward damaged tissues within minutes after trauma occurs. Agent1 reasoned based on understanding initial steps in wound healing involving rapid leukocyte infiltration for immediate defense mechanisms upon tissue insult. To summarize concisely:

The earliest transient change post-tissue injury reflects neutrophil activation initiating local defenses; this mirrors quick mobilization seen immediately after harm happens. Neutrophils move swiftly to injured sites starting around minute timescales aiding cleanup and protection from invaders right away during recovery processes initiated by physical disruption or insults affecting body parts like skin or organs where such responses occur naturally when needed most acutely without delay due nature’s design ensuring survival benefits through prompt action taken locally near point-of-damage initiation itself.
